past_medical_history: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma status post radical left nephrectomy 3 years ago, completed adjuvant immunotherapy 4 months ago
Coronary artery disease with two-vessel disease on cardiac catheterization 5 years ago, managed medically
Chronic kidney disease stage 3b (baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL)
History of pulmonary thromboembolism 2 years ago, completed 6 months of anticoagulation
Colonic polyps, status post polypectomy (tubular adenomas)

medications.indication: Coronary artery disease and rate control

family_history: Father died of sudden cardiac death at age 64
Mother had hypertension and chronic kidney disease, died at age 81 of heart failure
Brother had a pacemaker placed at age 70 for sick sinus syndrome

social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a ground-floor apartment; daughter visits daily
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 1 mile daily, but over the past 6 weeks has been limited by increasing fatigue and lightheadedness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate and prosody, no dysarthria. Attention intact — able to recite months of the year backwards without error. Recalls 3/3 words at 5 minutes. No confusion or post-ictal features observed at time of examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Fundoscopic exam without papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Facial sensation intact in all three divisions bilaterally. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal. Tone normal in all four extremities without rigidity, spasticity, or cogwheeling. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups bilaterally including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, hip flexors, quadriceps, hamstrings, ankle dorsiflexors, and plantarflexors. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact in upper extremities. Mildly diminished vibration sense at bilateral great toes consistent with mild peripheral neuropathy. Proprioception intact at toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ bilaterally. Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact. No intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No ataxic features.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration, no oral trauma. No evidence of urinary incontinence. No bruising suggesting prolonged convulsive activity. Bilateral rhonchi on lung auscultation. Small contusion on right temporal area from fall. No meningeal signs — neck supple, Kernig and Brudzinski signs negative.

patient.chief_complaint: Dizziness, lightheadedness, and fall with witnessed loss of consciousness
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Harold Brennan is a 72-year-old man with a significant past medical history including clear cell renal cell carcinoma treated with radical nephrectomy and adjuvant pembrolizumab (completed 4 months ago), coronary artery disease, chronic kidney disease, and hypertension, who was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his daughter found him lying on the bathroom floor. According to the daughter, she had arrived for her daily visit at approximately 10:00 AM and found her father unresponsive on the tile floor. He regained consciousness within approximately 15 to 20 seconds and was immediately lucid, asking her what had happened. He denied any preceding chest pain, palpitations, or aura. There was no witnessed tonic-clonic activity, no tongue biting, and no urinary or fecal incontinence. Upon regaining consciousness, he was not confused and could recount events up until the moment he felt lightheaded and his vision went dark. He reports that over the preceding three to four weeks he has experienced recurrent episodes of dizziness and a sensation of nearly blacking out, occurring without any clear positional trigger. These episodes lasted only seconds but have been increasing in frequency, happening almost daily over the past week. He has also noticed progressive fatigue and mild exertional dyspnea, particularly when climbing the three steps to his apartment entrance, which he attributes to deconditioning. He denies fever, headache, vomiting, difficulty swallowing, focal weakness, numbness, balance problems, speech changes, or visual disturbances. He denies any bowel or bladder changes, autonomic symptoms such as excessive sweating or heat intolerance, or new sensory complaints. He has no prior history of seizures. His daughter notes he has seemed more tired over the past month and has been less active than usual. A review of his oncology records confirms his last pembrolizumab infusion was 4 months ago and his most recent CT surveillance showed no evidence of recurrent malignancy.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and well-organized, reactive to eye opening and closure
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-to-medium amplitude beta activity, symmetric and unremarkable
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness captured with vertex sharp transients; no abnormal sleep architecture

artifacts.description: Low-grade intermittent muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Produced mild diffuse slowing that resolved within 30 seconds of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at standard flash frequencies (1-30 Hz). Normal photic driving response at mid-range frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response elicited.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG study. No epileptiform discharges, no focal slowing, and no seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording. The normal EEG does not support a diagnosis of epilepsy but does not exclude it. Clinical correlation is advised.
eeg.limitations: Routine recording; interictal sensitivity for epilepsy detection is approximately 50% for a single study. Prolonged or ambulatory EEG would increase sensitivity if clinical suspicion persists.
recommended_actions: If seizure remains a clinical concern, consider prolonged or ambulatory EEG monitoring
Correlate with cardiac evaluation given clinical presentation of transient loss of consciousness

ecg.rhythm: Complete heart block (third-degree atrioventricular block) with junctional escape rhythm
findings: Complete AV dissociation with independent atrial rate of approximately 82 bpm (sinus P waves visible)
Junctional/ventricular escape rhythm at 38 bpm with wide QRS morphology
Left bundle branch block morphology in escape beats
No acute ST elevation to suggest STEMI
Diffuse nonspecific ST-T wave changes, likely rate-related
ecg.interpretation: Third-degree (complete) atrioventricular block with slow junctional escape rhythm at 38 beats per minute. Complete AV dissociation is confirmed by P waves marching through QRS complexes at an independent rate. Wide QRS with left bundle branch block morphology. Borderline QTc prolongation. This is a hemodynamically significant conduction abnormality that fully explains the patient's syncopal episode. Urgent cardiology consultation and preparation for temporary pacing followed by permanent pacemaker implantation is recommended.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Complete heart block with slow escape rhythm is the most probable cause of this patient's syncope. The low ventricular rate results in inadequate cardiac output and cerebral hypoperfusion, causing transient loss of consciousness consistent with a Stokes-Adams attack. The patient's known coronary artery disease and age favor degenerative conduction system disease as the underlying etiology.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mild anemia, likely related to chronic kidney disease
Low, consistent with anemia of chronic kidney disease

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with known CKD stage 3b and solitary kidney
Elevated at baseline, consistent with CKD 3b after nephrectomy
Mildly elevated, likely stress response; no known diabetes

Cardiac_markers.clinical_significance: Within normal limits; no evidence of acute myocardial injury. Serial monitoring recommended given bradycardia.
Elevated, suggesting cardiac dysfunction likely related to chronic severe bradycardia and underlying coronary artery disease

Metabolic.clinical_significance: Normal thyroid function; hypothyroidism not contributing to bradycardia

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results show no acute metabolic derangement to account for syncope. Electrolytes are normal, ruling out hypokalemia or hyperkalemia as contributors to conduction abnormality. BUN and creatinine are elevated at baseline consistent with known CKD stage 3b status post nephrectomy. Mild normocytic anemia consistent with chronic kidney disease. Troponin is normal; however, BNP is significantly elevated suggesting underlying cardiac dysfunction, likely from chronic bradycardia. TSH is normal. No metabolic cause identified for the syncopal episode.
abnormal_values_summary: Hemoglobin: 12.4 g/dL (ref: 13.5-17.5) - Mild anemia, likely related to chronic kidney disease
Hematocrit: 36.8% (ref: 38.3-48.6) - Low, consistent with anemia of chronic kidney disease
BUN: 34 mg/dL (ref: 7-20) - Elevated, consistent with known CKD stage 3b and solitary kidney
Creatinine: 1.7 mg/dL (ref: 0.7-1.3) - Elevated at baseline, consistent with CKD 3b
Glucose: 102 mg/dL (ref: 70-100) - Mildly elevated, likely stress response
BNP: 312 pg/mL (ref: <100) - Elevated, suggesting cardiac dysfunction

Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Moderately reduced systolic function, likely related to chronic bradycardia and underlying coronary artery disease
Mildly dilated, consistent with chronic volume overload from bradycardia
Increased, consistent with longstanding hypertensive heart disease

Echocardiogram.value: Moderate sclerosis with mild stenosis (mean gradient 18 mmHg, AVA 1.6 cm2)
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Degenerative calcific aortic valve disease; calcification may extend into the conduction system contributing to heart block

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates moderately reduced left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 42%) with concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with hypertensive heart disease. Mild left atrial dilation. Moderate aortic valve sclerosis with mild stenosis is noted, and calcific degeneration may be extending into the conduction system, providing a structural basis for complete heart block. Mild functional mitral regurgitation. No intracardiac thrombus or masses. No pericardial effusion.
abnormal_values_summary: Left atrial diameter: 4.1 cm - Mildly dilated
IVS thickness: 1.3 cm - Increased, hypertensive heart disease
Aortic valve: Moderate sclerosis with mild stenosis - May contribute to conduction disease

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block with junctional escape rhythm throughout 24-hour recording
findings: Persistent complete AV block throughout the entire 24-hour recording period
Independent atrial rate averaging 80 bpm with regular sinus rhythm
Minimum ventricular rate 30 bpm at 04:15 AM with a 3.8-second RR pause
Maximum ventricular rate 44 bpm during brief ambulation at 14:20
No sustained ventricular tachycardia; 2 isolated 3-beat runs of non-sustained VT
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitoring confirms persistent complete (third-degree) AV block with an unreliable junctional escape rhythm. Critically slow rates are documented overnight with pauses up to 3.8 seconds. Two brief runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia were captured, indicating electrical instability of the escape focus. This is a high-risk conduction disturbance that confirms the indication for permanent pacemaker implantation.
output.clinical_correlation: The persistent complete heart block with pauses and non-sustained VT represents a high-risk substrate for both recurrent syncope and potential sudden cardiac death. Permanent pacemaker implantation is urgently indicated. Given LVEF 42%, cardiac resynchronization therapy with pacemaker (CRT-P) should be considered.

Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Low baseline BP reflecting poor cardiac output from complete heart block
Significant orthostatic drop secondary to fixed low heart rate preventing cardiac output augmentation
Absent chronotropic response due to complete heart block

Tilt_table_test.value: Lightheadedness and near-syncope at 2 minutes 30 seconds, test terminated early
Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Symptoms reproduced by hemodynamic compromise from fixed bradycardia
No cardioinhibitory or vasodepressor vasovagal mechanism identified

output.interpretation: Tilt table test does not demonstrate a vasovagal mechanism. Significant orthostatic hypotension is present but is secondary to the patient's underlying complete heart block with a fixed, low ventricular rate preventing appropriate cardiac output augmentation. Vasovagal syncope is ruled out. The primary etiology of syncope remains cardiac conduction disease.
abnormal_values_summary: Baseline BP: 92/54 mmHg - Low from poor cardiac output
Baseline HR: 38 bpm - Complete heart block
Tilt BP: 74/44 mmHg - Significant orthostatic drop from fixed rate
Tilt HR: 39 bpm - No chronotropic response

findings.location: Periventricular and deep white matter bilaterally
findings.size: Scattered punctate and small confluent foci

additional_observations: No acute infarction on DWI/ADC sequences
Mild generalized cortical volume loss appropriate for age
output.impression: Brain MRI is unremarkable for acute intracranial pathology. Chronic small vessel ischemic disease consistent with the patient's age and cardiovascular risk factors. No acute infarction, hemorrhage, mass, or demyelinating disease. No leptomeningeal enhancement to suggest meningeal carcinomatosis or inflammatory process. Brain MRI does not reveal a neurological cause for the patient's syncope.
recommended_actions: Direct evaluation toward cardiac etiology for syncope

output.query: cardiac syncope complete heart block management pacemaker

results.title: 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay
results.authors: Kusumoto FM, Schoenfeld MH, Barrett C, et al.
results.journal: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
results.summary: Complete AV block with symptomatic bradycardia is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation. In patients with LVEF ≤50% and expected ventricular pacing >40%, cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) should be considered over conventional RV pacing.

results.title: Syncope Due to Cardiac Arrhythmias: Diagnosis and Management
results.authors: Brignole M, Moya A, de Lange FJ, et al.
results.summary: Cardiac syncope from complete heart block (Stokes-Adams attacks) carries a high risk of recurrent events and sudden cardiac death. ECG is the single most important diagnostic test. Permanent pacing eliminates syncope in >95% of patients with AV block.

results.title: Distinguishing Cardiac Syncope from Epileptic Seizures in the Emergency Department
results.authors: Sheldon R, Rose S, Ritchie D, et al.
results.summary: Key features favoring cardiac syncope over seizure include: absence of tonic-clonic activity, absence of post-ictal confusion, rapid return to baseline, no tongue biting or incontinence, and presence of prodromal lightheadedness or palpitations.

output.summary: Complete heart block is a well-established cause of cardiac syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks). ACC/AHA guidelines recommend permanent pacemaker implantation as a Class I indication for symptomatic complete AV block. In patients with reduced LVEF and anticipated high ventricular pacing burden, CRT should be considered. The key clinical distinction from epileptic seizures is the absence of post-ictal confusion and rapid recovery.

interactions: Metoprolol slows AV conduction and suppresses automaticity of escape rhythms, potentially worsening bradycardia in the setting of complete heart block
Combined with amlodipine, there is additive negative chronotropic and negative inotropic effect
contraindications: Beta-blockers are relatively contraindicated in the setting of symptomatic complete heart block without a functioning pacemaker
Risk of hemodynamic collapse if escape rhythm is further suppressed
warnings: Do NOT abruptly discontinue metoprolol — however, in this acute setting with symptomatic complete heart block, holding the medication is appropriate
Ensure transcutaneous pacing capability is available if metoprolol is continued
output.formulary_status: Formulary, but contraindicated in current clinical setting
alternatives: Hold all AV-nodal blocking agents until permanent pacemaker is implanted
After pacemaker implantation, beta-blocker therapy may be safely resumed for coronary artery disease management

findings.location: Basal interventricular septum at the level of the membranous septum and bundle of His

signal_characteristics.T1: Elevated native T1 values (1120 ms) in the basal septum
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Mid-wall linear enhancement in the basal interventricular septum consistent with non-ischemic fibrosis in the conduction system region
Thin subendocardial enhancement consistent with prior small inferior myocardial infarction

additional_observations: Aortic valve calcification extending toward the intervalvular fibrosa
No features of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy
No evidence of cardiac sarcoidosis or amyloid infiltration
No myocardial edema on T2 mapping — no evidence of acute myocarditis
output.impression: Cardiac MRI reveals non-ischemic mid-wall fibrosis in the basal interventricular septum at the level of the conduction system, consistent with idiopathic degenerative conduction system disease (Lenegre-Lev disease). This provides the structural substrate for the patient's complete heart block. Evidence of a small prior inferior myocardial infarction. Aortic valve calcification extending toward the conduction system region may be a contributing factor. No evidence of sarcoidosis, amyloidosis, or acute myocarditis. LVEF 42%.
recommended_actions: Permanent pacemaker implantation for symptomatic complete heart block
Consider CRT-P given LVEF 42% and anticipated high ventricular pacing burden
Continue medical therapy for coronary artery disease

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Mid-wall septal fibrosis at the conduction system level with aortic valve calcification in an elderly patient with coronary disease

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Ischemic contribution to conduction system damage
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Prior inferior MI may have contributed to conduction system injury

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block

differential.key_distinguishing: No tonic-clonic activity witnessed, no post-ictal confusion, no tongue biting, no incontinence, normal EEG. Immediate return to full alertness after event.
No identifiable trigger such as pain, prolonged standing, or emotional stress. No prodromal nausea or diaphoresis. Age and cardiac history favor cardiac etiology.
Episodes are not positionally triggered, occur regardless of posture. Any orthostatic changes are secondary to the fixed low heart rate from complete heart block.

Last immunotherapy infusion was 4 months ago. No troponin elevation, no myocardial edema on imaging, normal neurological exam, normal EEG background. Immune-related adverse events typically occur during or shortly after therapy.
No focal neurological deficits, TIA rarely causes true loss of consciousness. Normal neurological exam and normal brain MRI.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to evaluate for arrhythmia or conduction disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complete heart block with junctional escape rhythm at 38 bpm

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous telemetry monitoring and establish IV access
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Ongoing bradycardia with complete AV dissociation

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, TSH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No metabolic cause for syncope; BNP elevated suggesting cardiac dysfunction; troponin normal

optimal_actions.action: Hold metoprolol and all AV-nodal blocking agents immediately
Apply transcutaneous pacing pads and have atropine available for symptomatic bradycardia
Urgent cardiology consultation for temporary pacing and permanent pacemaker planning
Obtain EEG to rule out epileptic seizure as a cause of transient loss of consciousness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG, no epileptiform abnormalities, supporting cardiac rather than epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to assess structural heart disease and ejection fraction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced LVEF, aortic valve sclerosis/stenosis, LV hypertrophy

optimal_actions.action: Admit to telemetry unit or CCU for monitoring pending pacemaker implantation
Plan for permanent pacemaker implantation (CRT-P preferred given reduced LVEF)

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to identify complete heart block
Hold metoprolol and all AV-nodal blocking agents immediately
Apply transcutaneous pacing pads and have atropine ready
Recognize that syncope is cardiac in origin and not a seizure or immune-related complication
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs for syncope misdiagnosed as seizure
Continuing or increasing beta-blocker dose, which would further suppress AV conduction
Administering IV adenosine, which would worsen AV block
Discharging the patient without cardiac monitoring and pacemaker evaluation
Attributing syncope to prior immunotherapy without evidence of immune-mediated cardiac or neurological complication
Performing lumbar puncture without clear indication for CNS infection or inflammation
key_reasoning_points: Sudden loss of consciousness without warning or prodrome is characteristic of cardiac syncope (Stokes-Adams attack), not vasovagal or epileptic events
Immediate return to full alertness without post-ictal confusion strongly favors cardiac syncope over seizure
Absence of tonic-clonic activity, tongue biting, and incontinence argues against epileptic seizure
The ECG showing complete heart block with junctional escape at 38 bpm provides the definitive diagnosis and hemodynamic mechanism
The patient's history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, and age suggests degenerative conduction system disease (Lenegre-Lev disease) as the underlying etiology

The history of pembrolizumab therapy is a potential red herring — immune checkpoint inhibitor-related myocarditis can cause conduction abnormalities, but the 4-month interval since last dose and absence of troponin elevation or myocardial edema make this unlikely
Metoprolol is an AV-nodal blocking agent that may be worsening the bradycardia and must be held immediately
Normal EEG excludes an epileptic etiology and supports the cardiac mechanism for transient loss of consciousness
Elevated BNP reflects chronic cardiac dysfunction from sustained severe bradycardia, reinforcing the need for permanent pacing

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic presentation of sudden syncope without warning in an elderly male with cardiac risk factors. ECG clearly shows complete heart block. The history of prior pembrolizumab therapy provides a minor distracting element but findings clearly point to degenerative conduction disease.
